core_model.structure_summary: A continuous-time interacting-particle Markov jump process on a finite Cartesian lattice in which each site holds a binary state that transitions irreversibly from 0 to 1 at a rate equal to a constant spontaneous term plus a term proportional to the count of already-transitioned nearest neighbors.
core_model.canonical_form: X_{\mathbf{j}}(t)\in\{0,1\},\; X_{\mathbf{j}}(0)=0;\quad \mathbb{P}(X_{\mathbf{j}}(t+\Delta t)=1\mid \mathbf{X}(t))=\left(p+\tfrac{q}{2D}N_{\mathbf{j}}(t)\right)\Delta t \text{ if } X_{\mathbf{j}}(t)=0,\; N_{\mathbf{j}}=\sum_{i}(X_{\mathbf{j}-\hat e_i}+X_{\mathbf{j}+\hat e_i});\quad \text{aggregate } f(t)=\tfrac{1}{M}\sum_{\mathbf{j}}\mathbb{E}X_{\mathbf{j}}(t)

variables.meaning: per-site binary indicator that has crossed from 0 to 1 (absorbing at 1)
continuous time of the jump process
D-dimensional Cartesian lattice site index, with edges/boundaries central
neighbor-coupling rate scaling the count of transitioned nearest neighbors

notes: Core is the discrete Bass model as a lattice interacting-particle system; the paper's results are boundary corrections to the aggregate transitioned-fraction, so boundary_conditions_central is genuinely load-bearing. Closed-form 1D expression f=1-exp(-(p+q)t+(q/p)(1-e^{-pt})) is a derived quantity, not the core object. A separate panel-regression statistical model (last equation) is an auxiliary empirical tool, not the core.
